---
pagetitle: "Geometry-driven complexity"
page-layout: full
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

```{=html}


<div class="research-subpage">

<section class="research-subpage-intro">
  <div class="research-kicker">Pattern formation · Hyperuniformity · Emergent order</div>
  <h2 class="research-title">Geometry-driven complexity</h2>
  <div class="research-lede">
    <h3><i>How can simple geometric interactions generate large-scale order?</i></h3>
  </div>
</section>


<section class="research-subpage-section">
  <h2 class="research-section-title">Overview</h2>
  <p>
    This research theme traces my entry into complexity science through geometry-driven hyperuniformity and related questions of pattern formation. It asks how local geometric interactions, constraints, and relaxation rules can generate large-scale order.
  </p>

  <h2 class="research-section-title">Interactive demo</h2>
  <p>
    The interactive Lloyd demo provides an intuition-building way to explore how local relaxation dynamics can transform point configurations and their emergent structure.
  </p>
  <a class="demo-button" href="../interactive/lloyd/">Launch interactive demo →</a>
</section>



<section class="research-band">
  <div class="research-subpage-section">
    <h2 class="research-section-title">Relevant publications</h2>
    <p class="research-muted">
      This list is intended for publications connected to geometry-driven hyperuniformity, pattern formation, local relaxation, and emergent order.
    </p>

    <div class="publication-list">
    
      <article class="publication-item">
        <a class="publication-snapshot-link" href="https://doi.org/10.1142/S3029101125500012">
					<img class="publication-snapshot" src="../images/publications/data_driven_modelling.jpg" alt="Snapshot image for DDM 2025">
				</a>
        <div class="publication-detail">
        	<a class="title-link" href="https://doi.org/10.1142/S3029101125500012">
        		<h3>Scale-free correlations in two-dimensional geometry-driven hyperuniformity</h3>
        	</a>
          <div class="publication-meta">
            <strong>Authors:</strong> <u>Sungyeon Hong</u>, Mohammad Saadatfar<br>
            <strong>Year:</strong> 2025<br>
            <strong>Publisher:</strong> Data-Driven Modelling: Unravelling Complexity in the Sea of Data
          </div>
          <p class="publication-description">
            This work investigates how large-scale order can emerge from simple local interactions. Using a computational process known as Lloyd's algorithm, we show that a disordered collection of points can self-organise into a hyperuniform state characterised by orientational domains whose sizes grow with the system itself. The absence of a characteristic length scale suggests a form of scale-free organisation, a hallmark of systems capable of coordinated collective responses across many scales.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>

      <article class="publication-item">
        <a class="publication-snapshot-link" href="https://doi.org/10.1093/pnasnexus/pgae510">
					<img class="publication-snapshot" src="../images/publications/pnas_nexus.jpeg" alt="Snapshot image for PNAS Nexus 2024">
				</a>
        <div class="publication-detail">
        	<a class="title-link" href="https://doi.org/10.1093/pnasnexus/pgae510">
          	<h3>Topological mechanical states in geometry-driven hyperuniform materials</h3>
          </a>
          <div class="publication-meta">
            <strong>Authors:</strong> <u>Sungyeon Hong</u>, Can Nerse, Sebastian Oberst, Mohammad Saadatfar<br>
            <strong>Year:</strong> 2024<br>
            <strong>Publisher:</strong> PNAS Nexus
          </div>
          <p class="publication-description">
            Can disorder be engineered to produce useful physical behaviour? In this study, we reveal how topological defects that emerge during the formation of hyperuniform structures give rise to distinct mechanical states capable of localising vibrational energy. By connecting geometry, topology, and mechanics, this work demonstrates how robust functional properties can arise from self-organising disordered materials.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      
      <article class="publication-item" style="--publication-border: 15px solid #FFFFFF;">
        <a class="publication-snapshot-link" href="https://doi.org/10.1051/epjconf/202124915002">
					<img class="publication-snapshot" src="../images/publications/powders_and_grains.png" alt="Snapshot image for P&G 2021">
				</a>
        <div class="publication-detail">
          <a class="title-link" href="https://doi.org/10.1051/epjconf/202124915002">
          	<h3>Dynamical arrest of topological defects in 2D hyperuniform disk packings</h3>
          </a>
          <div class="publication-meta">
            <strong>Authors:</strong> <u>Sungyeon Hong</u>, Michael A. Klatt, Gerd Schröder-Turk, Nicolas François, and Mohammad Saadatfar<br>
            <strong>Year:</strong> 2021<br>
            <strong>Conference:</strong> Powders & Grains 2021 – 9th International Conference on Micromechanics on Granular Media
          </div>
          <p class="publication-description">
            This paper explores how topological defects evolve during the formation of hyperuniform structures. We show that as the system becomes increasingly ordered, defects slow down and eventually become trapped, leaving a characteristic structural signature. The results provide insight into how local topological processes shape the emergence of large-scale order in complex systems.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>


</div>

```
